title: The Middle East during and after WWI
description: Check your understanding of the role of World War I in shaping the Middle East.
topic_path: World War I shapes the Middle East
The Middle East during and after WWI

assessment_items.question_content:
True or false:  In January of 1919, King Faisal (son of Hussein bin Ali) agreed to support Jewish settlement in Palestine if, in return, the World Zionist Organization helped the Arabs secure an independent Arab state.

[[☃ radio 1]]

Which of the following is **not** true of the Middle East after World War I?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
In the early 1900s and through WWI, the Arabian Peninsula was nominally under Ottoman rule but was really controlled by various tribal leaders.  Hussein bin Ali, the Sherif of Mecca, was the leading figure in the Hejaz (the Western portion of the Peninsula containing the holy cities of Mecca and Medina) who helped the British against the Ottomans in the Arab Revolt.  He declared himself King of the Hejaz after WWI.

Abdulaziz (known as Ibn Saud) came to power in the Nejd (the central part of the Peninsula including Riyadh in the early 1900s and consolidated his power during WWI (he was not active in the Arab Revolt with the British).  By 1926, he was able to take control of the Hijaz from Hussein bin Ali and declare himself King of Nejd and Hejaz.  This was formally merged into the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia in 1932.

choices.content: Modern-day Jordan and Iraq emerged from WWI as British Mandates (essentially under British control)
Modern-day Lebanon and Syria emerged from WWI as French Mandates (essentially under French control)
Egyptians won their independence from the British in 1922 through a successful revolt
Kuwait didn't get independence from Britain until 1961
Saudi Arabia was created by Hussein bin Ali after he helped the British against the Ottomans in the Hejaz and Palestine

assessment_items.question_content:
Which of the following is **not** true of the Arabs during and after World War I?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The Balfour Declaration called for British support for a Jewish national home in Palestine.  This caused many in the Arab community to question whether Britain was sincere in its promises made to the Arab leadership prior to the Arab Revolt (which, by most interpretations, included Palestine in a future independent Arab state).

choices.content: They were angered by the Sykes-Picot Treaty between Britain and France that divided much of the Middle East between them
They helped the British fight against the Ottomans in the Palestine Front
The Arab Revolt began in 1916 and was led by the Sharif of Mecca
They embraced the Balfour Declaration because it helped ensure an independent Arab State
They did not get the independent state after the war that was promised in the McMahon-Hussein Correspondence

assessment_items.question_content:
Which of the following is depicted in this map?

![Map of the Middle East](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2291e78cb2fd7ce072cf61c2e9ff35b6.jpg)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
This map shows the general partition agreed upon between Britain and France in the secret 1916 Sykes-Picot Agreement.  It showed French control and/or influence in the Blue areas and area A.  British control and/or influence in the Pink areas and area B.  This secret agreement was counter to the open assurances that the British had made to the Arabs when convincing them to rise up against the Ottomans (the Arabs were expecting an independent state in much of the territory partitioned in Sykes Picot).

In the aftermath of WWI, the territory was partitioned into French and British mandates not too different than those described in Sykes-Picot.

choices.content: State boundaries under Ottoman rule in 1914
The 1917 agreement between Germany and Russia partitioning the Middle East
The secret Sykes-Picot agreement in 1916 between France and Britain
The territory divisions articulated by the Balfour Declaration in 1917
Partitions of the Middle East created by the Treaty of Versailles with the blue area going to the British

assessment_items.question_content:
Which of the following gave the Arabs during World War I confidence that the British would give them an independent state that would have comprised the modern-day states of Syria, Iraq, Jordan and Israel after the War.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The McMahon-Hussein Correspondence  gave the Arabs during World War I confidence that the British would give them an independent state.

In the McMahon-Hussein Correspondence was an exchange of letters over 1915 and 1916 in which the British High Commissioner in Egypt, Sir Henry McMahon, reassured the Sherif of Mecca, Hussein bin Ali that the Arabs would have an independent state comprising the Palestine, Mesopotamia and Syria in order to covince them to join the British against the Ottomans.

assessment_items.question_content:
By articulating the need for a Jewish state in 1895 in *Der Judenstaat (The Jewish State)*, he became the father of political Zionism and the first head of the Zionist Organization (which later became the World Zionist Organization).

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Theodor Herzl wrote *Der Judenstaat* and was the first head of the Zionist Organization.

assessment_items.question_content:
What British officer was best known for his role in helping convince the Arabs to rise up against the Ottoman Empire and fought alongside the Arabs during World War I?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: T.E. Lawrence was a British archaeologist in the Middle East who could speak Arabic.  Shortly after the outbreak of war in 1914, he enlisted in the British Army where he was posted to the intelligence staff in Cairo.  By mid-1916, he was working with Arab forces in the Hejaz (west side of the Arabian Peninsula where Mecca and Medina are located) in their revolt against the Ottoman Empire.

assessment_items.question_content:
True or false:  With the help of Indian troops in Mesopotamia and Arabs in the Hejaz and Palestine, Allied troops were able to occupy most of the Middle East between the Mediterranean Sea and Persia by the end of World War I.

[[☃ radio 1]]
